mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of Elastic Stack components on Kubernetes. It is ideal for users looking to run Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic services in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add the Elastic Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Elastic Helm repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install ECK.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Elastic Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are required for ECK to manage Elastic Stack resources. This step installs them into your cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://download.elastic.co/downloads/eck/2.14.0/crds.yaml

```

> This command installs the necessary CRDs for ECK.

steps.title: Install the ECK operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator using Helm, which will manage the Elastic Stack resources.

```bash

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --create-namespace --version 2.14.0

```

> Installs the ECK operator in the elastic-system namespace.

steps.title: Create an ElasticSearch cluster
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a Custom Resource definition.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.6.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

```

> This command creates a 3-node Elasticsearch cluster.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.6.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Elasticsearch cluster to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n default

```

> This command lists the Elasticsearch resources in the default namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the ECK operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all managed Elastic Stack resources, leading to data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace where the operator was installed.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the ECK operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data. This step upgrades the ECK operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n default -o json > elasticsearch-backup.json

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

kubectl get elasticsearch -n default

```

> Backs up the current Elasticsearch configuration.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the Elasticsearch resources are still running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get elasticsearch -n default -o json > elasticsearch-backup.json

troubleshooting.title: Elasticsearch cluster is not starting.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to resource constraints or misconfiguration.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Elasticsearch pods using `kubectl logs <pod-name>` and adjust resource requests/limits.

troubleshooting.title: ECK operator pod is crashing.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the operator is unable to connect to the Kubernetes API.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the operator has the correct permissions and check the RBAC configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to access Kibana.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kibana may not be exposed correctly or the service is not running.

**Fix:**
Verify the Kibana service is running and check the service type (LoadBalancer/NodePort).

troubleshooting.title: Custom Resource not found.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the CRDs were not installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using `kubectl apply -f https://download.elastic.co/downloads/eck/2.14.0/crds.yaml`.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x, improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 2.13, ensure to update your Elasticsearch configurations to comply with the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'Green' when running `kubectl get elasticsearch -n default`.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.6.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources.
